---
title: "Foundations Episode 5 — Reproducible Environments"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Explain why "works on my machine" is a bug, not a defence.
- Isolate dependencies with a virtual environment.
- Pin exact versions with a lockfile so a build is repeatable.
- Capture a whole environment in a container and a dev container.

**Teaching:** 45 min · **Exercises:** 30 min
:::

## Why reproducibility?

A result you can't reproduce isn't a result, and a robot stack that only runs on *your* laptop can't
be deployed. The enemy is hidden state: a package version that drifted, a system library that happens
to be installed, an environment variable only you have. Reproducibility means anyone — a teammate, a
CI runner, a robot — gets the **same** environment from the **same** declaration. This is the muscle
that CI/CD (Episode 4) and the whole Cloud track depend on.

## Virtual environments — isolate dependencies

Never install project packages into the system Python. Create an isolated environment per project:

```bash
python3 -m venv .venv          # create
source .venv/bin/activate      # activate (your shell prompt changes)
pip install numpy matplotlib
deactivate                     # leave it
```

Now this project's packages can't collide with another project's — or with the OS.

## Pin exact versions — the lockfile

"Install numpy" is not reproducible: next month "numpy" is a different version. **Pin** what you
actually used so the next person gets exactly that:

```bash
pip freeze > requirements.txt      # record EXACT versions
# numpy==1.26.4
# matplotlib==3.8.2
pip install -r requirements.txt    # anyone reproduces your environment
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
Show the difference between `numpy` and `numpy==1.26.4` in a `requirements.txt`. Ask the class what
happens to a six-month-old experiment when the unpinned version silently major-bumps. Pinning is the
cheapest reproducibility win there is — make them feel why before moving to containers.
:::

## Containers — capture the whole machine

A virtual environment pins Python packages; a **container** pins the operating system, system
libraries, and tools too. You declare the environment in a `Dockerfile`:

```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt    # reproducible: same lockfile, same image
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "robot_node.py"]
```

```bash
docker build -t robot-node .
docker run --rm robot-node             # runs identically on any machine with Docker
```

The image *is* the environment — the same bits run on your laptop, a CI runner, and the robot.

## Dev containers — reproducibility for development too

A **dev container** applies the same idea to your editor: everyone opens the project in an identical,
pre-provisioned environment. This repository ships one — see
[`.devcontainer/`](https://github.com/FrankAsanteVanLaarhoven/ForgeLabCarpentries/blob/main/.devcontainer/README.md),
which gives every contributor ROS 2 + Quarto with no "install these 14 things first" page.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 1 — Why pin versions?
Your analysis ran fine in January with `requirements.txt` containing just `pandas`. In June the same
command errors out. What most likely changed, and what one edit prevents it?

### Solution
`pandas` released a new version between January and June, and an unpinned `pip install` pulled the
newer, behaviour-changed release. Pinning the exact version (`pandas==2.1.4`) — ideally via
`pip freeze` — locks the environment so June reproduces January.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge 2 — venv or container?
A collaborator can reproduce your Python packages but still gets different results because they're on
a different OS with a different system library. Which tool fixes this, and why?

### Solution
A **container**. A virtual environment only isolates Python packages; it inherits the host OS and
system libraries. A container captures the OS and system dependencies too, so the *whole* environment
— not just the Python layer — is identical across machines.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- "Works on my machine" is hidden state — reproducibility removes it.
- Use a **virtual environment** to isolate a project's Python packages.
- **Pin exact versions** in a lockfile so a build repeats months later.
- **Containers** capture the whole machine; **dev containers** do the same for development.
:::

This completes the Foundations track. Next, apply these habits in the Robotics track:
[Episode 3 — Robot Navigation with ROS 2](03-ros2-navigation.qmd).
